title: Timepoint 1 — Discharge day
scenarioBrief: Lan Nguyen is a 72-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman who was admitted on 2/15/2027 with severe gallstone pancreatitis. Her course was complicated by septic shock, ARDS requiring intubation, DVT, and a 57-day hospital stay. She is preparing for discharge to a rehab facility today (4/12/2027). On a final review of imaging before discharge, a 2.5 cm right upper lobe lung nodule that was incidentally noted on her sepsis-workup chest X-ray (and confirmed on follow-up CT) is re-noticed — it has not appeared in her daily progress notes since hospital day 8.

chartGate._notes: TP1 visibility: complete chart through discharge day — historical PCP visits at Charm City Asian Health Center, prior orthopedic record (R TKA 2018), prior infectious disease workup (positive PPD 2010), prior US confirming cholelithiasis (2025), admission H&P, ICU consults, daily progress notes (which dropped the lung nodule after HD 8), CXR and CT reports, pulmonary nodule consult, TB rule-out (AFB + NAAT all negative), DVT consult, rehab planning.

prompts.question: Based on the information you have at this time, what is your differential diagnosis for this incidental lung nodule?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Differential diagnosis for the incidental lung nodule. Award 1 point for EACH category included on the differential (6 items, 1 point each; max 6):
a) Primary lung malignancy
b) Metastasis to the lung
c) Tuberculosis
d) Non-TB primary lung infection (e.g., bacterial pneumonia; fungal / endemic mycoses such as histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis)
e) Rheumatologic / autoimmune nodule (e.g., rheumatoid nodule, granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
f) Benign tumor / granuloma / calcified scar
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 6.

prompts.question: What additional information (from the patient, the chart, or non-invasive workup) could you obtain that would help further hone the differential diagnosis for this lung nodule?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Additional non-invasive information to further hone the differential. Award points as follows (max 9):
- Detailed smoking history (pack-years, time since quit): 1
- Environmental / occupational exposure history (e.g., textile cotton dust, asbestos, radon): 1
- Personal or family cancer history (especially the brother's lung cancer, sister's breast cancer): 1
- Prior history of lung infections: 1
- Prior TB testing, treatment, or BCG vaccination status: 1
- Nodule imaging features — TWO OF THREE of {spiculation; solid vs subsolid character; upper-lobe location}: 2
- ONE OF TWO of {history of rheumatologic/autoimmune disease; geography of current and previous living (regions endemic for histo/cocci/blasto)}: 1
- ONE OF TWO of {hemoptysis; lymphadenopathy}: 1
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 9.

prompts.question: The patient is being planned for discharge today and is expecting to leave today to go to rehab. What factors would influence your decision about keeping her in the hospital for additional workup vs. deferring to the outpatient setting?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Factors influencing keep-in-hospital vs. defer-to-outpatient for the nodule workup. Award points as follows (max 11):
- Patient's understanding of her current condition (including limits imposed by the language barrier): 1
- Patient's understanding of the importance of follow-up: 1
- Patient's ability to navigate the healthcare system (language, insurance, transportation): 1
- Family / caregiver support (specifically the role of the son and daughter-in-law): 1
- Quality / effectiveness of the health system's operations for lung-nodule patients (nodule clinic? tracking mechanism?): 1
- Risk and cost of additional inpatient days (deconditioning, nosocomial infection, financial burden): 1
- Plan for anticoagulation around any biopsy (currently on apixaban for the new DVT): 1
- Whether a biopsy plan is in place: 1
- ONE OF TWO of {risk tolerance of the patient; risk tolerance of the clinician}: 1
- TWO OF FOUR of {outpatient/PCP access; location/distance of and ease of communication with the rehab facility; ability to attend outpatient specialist appointments while at rehab; timeliness of outpatient specialty appointments}: 2
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 11.

prompts.question: If you were to defer the workup to the outpatient setting, what would be your process? Be concrete.

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Concrete process for deferring the workup to the outpatient setting. Award 1 point for EACH (max 7):
- Ensure patient and family understanding of the current condition (in Vietnamese, with formal interpretation)
- Emphasize the importance of follow-up specifically because of concern for malignancy
- Schedule the outpatient biopsy (or pulmonary nodule clinic referral) BEFORE discharge — not left to the patient as an outpatient task
- Assess patient and family needs around discharge (transportation to follow-up, language, financial)
- Communicate the discharge recommendation to the outpatient care team (PCP, rehab facility medical director)
- Clear communication of the plan and anticipatory guidance to the patient/family in appropriate language
- Establish an anticoagulation plan around the biopsy (when to hold apixaban, when to resume)
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 7.

prompts.question: If you were to keep her in the hospital for additional investigations, at what point in those investigations would you discharge her?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
If she is kept in the hospital for the additional workup, at what point should she be discharged. Award 1 point for EACH (max 3):
- After the biopsy itself is completed
- After resumption of oral anticoagulation post-procedure (i.e., safely back on apixaban)
- After clear delineation of the outpatient care plan (results pathway, follow-up appointment booked, who is calling whom with the results)
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 3.
